subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Stress, Locus of Control, and Intelligence
stimulus:
Dr. Dawson is a psychologist who believes that students who read motivational statements before a test will
experience lower levels of anxiety as compared with students who read neutral statements. To examine this
belief, he conducted a study. In the study, he randomly assigned half of the participants to read motivational
statements and the other half to read neutral statements. Immediately after reading the statements, all of the
students completed a survey about their current level of anxiety (1 = low; 8 = high) and then took the test. Dr.
Dawson found statistically significant results that are depicted in the graph.

parts.prompt: State a valid operational definition of the dependent variable in this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that anxiety is defined by their score on the scale/survey of their  anxiety.

required_elements: The dependent variable can be operationally defined as the score on the anxiety scale.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable can be operationally defined as the score on the anxiety scale.

required_elements: The dependent variable is the score on the scale/survey.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is the score on the scale/survey.

required_elements: The dependent variable is the anxiety score from 1 to 8 (or 1 to 10).
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is the anxiety score from 1 to 8 (or 1 to 10).

wrong_examples: The operational definition is the level of anxiety.
The operational definition is the anxiety score.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that anxiety is defined by their score on the scale/survey of their anxiety.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable can be operationally defined as the score on the anxiety scale.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — references score on the scale as the operational definition.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the score on the scale/survey.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — names the score on the scale or survey.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the anxiety score from 1 to 8 (or 1 to 10).
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — includes both 'score' and the range.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition is the level of anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — does not mention the scale/survey.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition is the anxiety score.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — 'score' alone without naming the scale/survey does not qualify.

parts.prompt: Identify the control group in this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the control group is the group that reads the neutral  statements.

required_elements: The control group is the group that reads neutral statements.
alternatives.correct_example: The control group is the group that reads neutral statements.

required_elements: The control group is the group that does not read positive statements.
alternatives.correct_example: The control group is the group that does not read positive statements.

wrong_examples: The control group is the group that reads the positive statements.
The control group is the group that does not read the neutral statements.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the control group is the group that reads the neutral statements.

sample_responses.response: The control group is the group that reads neutral statements.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — directly names the neutral-statement group as the control.

sample_responses.response: The control group is the group that does not read positive statements.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — defines the control group by absence of the treatment.

sample_responses.response: The control group is the group that reads the positive statements.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — reverses the groups; positive/motivational is the experimental group.

sample_responses.response: The control group is the group that does not read the neutral statements.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — also reverses the groups (neutral group is control, not its complement).

parts.prompt: Explain why the study would be considered an experiment.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the study is experimental because an independent variable is  manipulated, and/or the participants were randomly assigned to the groups in the study.

required_elements: The study is experimental because the students are randomly assigned to two conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The study is experimental because the students are randomly assigned to two conditions.

required_elements: The study is an experiment because a variable was manipulated.
alternatives.correct_example: The study is an experiment because a variable was manipulated.

wrong_examples: The study is experimental because they were randomly chosen to participate.
The study is experimental because it tests a hypothesis.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the study is experimental because an independent variable is manipulated, and/or the participants were randomly assigned to the groups in the study.

sample_responses.response: The study is experimental because the students are randomly assigned to two conditions.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — identifies random assignment as the defining experimental feature.

sample_responses.response: The study is an experiment because a variable was manipulated.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — identifies IV manipulation as the defining feature.

sample_responses.response: The study is experimental because they were randomly chosen to participate.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — confuses random selection with random assignment.

sample_responses.response: The study is experimental because it tests a hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — many non-experimental designs also test hypotheses.

parts.prompt: Explain how the results of the study do or do not support Dr. Dawson's hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because either the  motivational statement group had lower anxiety or the neutral statement group had  higher anxiety.

required_elements: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had lower anxiety.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had lower anxiety.

required_elements: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the neutral statement group had higher anxiety.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the neutral statement group had higher anxiety.

wrong_examples: The results do not support Dr. Dawson's hypothesis.
Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had higher anxiety.
Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had low anxiety.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because either the motivational statement group had lower anxiety or the neutral statement group had higher anxiety.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had lower anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — states support and the directional difference (motivational < neutral).

sample_responses.response: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the neutral statement group had higher anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — same directional difference framed via the neutral group.

sample_responses.response: The results do not support Dr. Dawson's hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — contradicts the graph.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had higher anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — states the wrong direction of the effect.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Dawson's hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had low anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — 'low' rather than 'lower' fails to establish the comparative relationship.

parts.prompt: The test Dr. Dawson gave was a vocabulary test. Explain how the following terms might relate to the students who performed poorly on the test in this study. Explain how External locus of control relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that students may perform poorly because they believe that  factors outside of themselves (e.g., fate, luck, destiny, circumstances, other people) are  responsible for their performance.

required_elements: Students who believe that fate determines their scores on the test won't perform as well.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who believe that fate determines their scores on the test won't perform as well.

required_elements: Students who believe that the unfairness of their teacher determines their score won't pass the test.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who believe that the unfairness of their teacher determines their score won't pass the test.

wrong_examples: Students did badly because the questions were too hard.
Students believe they can control how they do on the test.
The students may think there is nothing they can do, so they perform worse on the test.
The students did poorly on the quiz because they believed they lacked the ability to do
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that students may perform poorly because they believe that factors outside of themselves (e.g., fate, luck, destiny, circumstances, other people) are responsible for their performance.

sample_responses.response: Students who believe that fate determines their scores on the test won't perform as well.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — belief that fate (external) determines performance.

sample_responses.response: Students who believe that the unfairness of their teacher determines their score won't pass the test.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — belief that another person (teacher) controls their outcome.

sample_responses.response: Students did badly because the questions were too hard.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — states a fact about the test, not a student belief.

sample_responses.response: Students believe they can control how they do on the test.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes internal locus of control, not external.

sample_responses.response: The students may think there is nothing they can do, so they perform worse on the test.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes helplessness without identifying a specific external factor.

sample_responses.response: The students did poorly on the quiz because they believed they lacked the ability to do well.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — attributes to perceived lack of ability (internal), not external factors.

parts.prompt: The test Dr. Dawson gave was a vocabulary test. Explain how the following terms might relate to the students who performed poorly on the test in this study. Explain how Stage one of Selye's general adaptation syndrome relates to the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: NOTE: Students are not required to identify the name of the stage in order to earn the point.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that the students' sympathetic nervous system or some specific  aspect of their sympathetic nervous system is activated which interferes with taking the  test. 

OR 

Response indicates that the students' fight-or-flight mechanism is activated, which  interferes with them taking the test. 

NOTE: Students are not required to identify the name of the stage in order to earn the  point.

required_elements: Students' fight or flight response might engage if they perceive the test as being a threat, so they might score lower than if they weren't stressed.
alternatives.correct_example: Students' fight or flight response might engage if they perceive the test as being a threat, so they might score lower than if they weren't stressed.

required_elements: Students' heart rates increased, and they performed poorly.
alternatives.correct_example: Students' heart rates increased, and they performed poorly.

wrong_examples: The student was too alarmed or too aroused to perform well on the test.
Students with anxiety might get sick because they've resisted stress so much.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the students' sympathetic nervous system or some specific aspect of their sympathetic nervous system is activated which interferes with taking the test. OR Response indicates that the students' fight-or-flight mechanism is activated, which interferes with them taking the test. NOTE: Students are not required to identify the name of the stage in order to earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Students' fight or flight response might engage if they perceive the test as being a threat, so they might score lower than if they weren't stressed.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — fight-or-flight activation tied to lower test performance.

sample_responses.response: Students' heart rates increased, and they performed poorly.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — specific sympathetic response (increased heart rate) linked to poor performance.

sample_responses.response: The student was too alarmed or too aroused to perform well on the test.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — emotional terms (alarmed, aroused) without a physiological element.

sample_responses.response: Students with anxiety might get sick because they've resisted stress so much.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes Stage 3 exhaustion, not Stage 1 alarm, and lacks link to poor performance on the test.

parts.prompt: The test Dr. Dawson gave was a vocabulary test. Explain how the following terms might relate to the students who performed poorly on the test in this study. Explain how Crystallized intelligence relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that a lack of crystallized intelligence (e.g., accumulated  knowledge, facts, general information, or vocabulary) could have hindered student  performance on the test.

required_elements: Students who study the wrong vocabulary perform poorly.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who study the wrong vocabulary perform poorly.

required_elements: Students who do not have prior knowledge do poorly on the test.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who do not have prior knowledge do poorly on the test.

wrong_examples: Students may not be able to come up with abstract answers quickly, so they score low on the test.
The memory of a past bad experience on a test makes them perform poorly on the current test.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that a lack of crystallized intelligence (e.g., accumulated knowledge, facts, general information, or vocabulary) could have hindered student performance on the test.

sample_responses.response: Students who study the wrong vocabulary perform poorly.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — lacking the right accumulated vocabulary knowledge yields poor performance.

sample_responses.response: Students who do not have prior knowledge do poorly on the test.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — insufficient prior accumulated knowledge hinders performance.

sample_responses.response: Students may not be able to come up with abstract answers quickly, so they score low on the test.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes fluid intelligence, not crystallized.

sample_responses.response: The memory of a past bad experience on a test makes them perform poorly on the vocabulary test.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — references unrelated emotional memory, not accumulated knowledge.
